mapQuestions.question: What is the capital of Poland?
mapQuestions.answer: Warsaw — a city rebuilt almost from scratch after being destroyed in WWII.

mapQuestions.question: How many countries border Poland?
mapQuestions.answer: Seven: Germany, Czech Republic, Slovakia, Ukraine, Belarus, Lithuania, and Russia (via Kaliningrad).

mapQuestions.question: What is the longest river in Poland?
mapQuestions.answer: The Vistula (Wisła) — runs from the Tatra mountains through Kraków and Warsaw to the Baltic.

mapQuestions.question: What is the famous old forest in north-east Poland where European bison still roam wild?
mapQuestions.answer: Białowieża Forest — one of Europe's last primeval forests, shared with Belarus.

foods.name: Pierogi
foods.description: Stuffed dumplings — fillings include potato-and-cheese (ruskie), meat, mushroom, or sweet cherry. Boiled then sometimes fried.

foods.name: Bigos
foods.description: Hunter's stew — sauerkraut, cabbage, smoked sausage, meat, dried mushrooms. The longer it cooks, the better.

foods.name: Żurek
foods.description: Sour rye soup with sausage and a halved boiled egg, sometimes served in a bowl made of bread.

foods.name: Polish sausage (kielbasa)
foods.description: Smoked sausage, often grilled with mustard and rye bread. Many regional varieties — krakowska, mysliwska, biała.

foods.name: Placki ziemniaczane
foods.description: Crispy fried potato pancakes, eaten with sour cream or sweet apple sauce.

foods.name: Sernik
foods.description: Polish cheesecake — denser and creamier than the American version, often made with twaróg (curd cheese).

scavengerItems.label: A castle or palace (Poland has hundreds)
An onion-domed Orthodox church or red-brick Gothic cathedral
An old-fashioned tram in the city centre
A bakery with at least 8 types of rye bread
Hand-made pierogi in a restaurant window
A forest with mushrooms (Poles forage in autumn)
A horse-drawn carriage in an old town square
Salt being mined or a salt sculpture (Wieliczka mine!)
A WWII memorial or candle on a street corner

geoMatches.description: The capital, almost completely rebuilt after WWII. Modern skyscrapers next to a meticulously reconstructed Old Town.
Poland's historic former capital, untouched by WWII bombing. Vast medieval square, Wawel Castle, and a dragon statue that breathes real fire.
A Baltic Sea port with brightly painted merchant houses, a long history of shipbuilding, and amber-shaped streetlamps.
High peaks shared with Slovakia. Skiing, hiking, and the resort town of Zakopane with wooden mountain chalets.
A 700-year-old salt mine near Kraków. Inside: chapels, statues, even chandeliers — all carved from salt. UNESCO-listed.

senses.smell: Fresh rye bread, woodsmoke, frying pierogi, autumn forest mushrooms, candle wax in churches…
senses.hear: Polish church bells, tram bells, accordion folk music, "Dzień dobry!" (good day) greetings…
senses.taste: Sour rye soup, buttery dumplings, smoky kielbasa, sharp horseradish, sweet hot tea with lemon…
senses.feel: Cold winter air on cheeks, rough cobblestones, soft wool sweaters, smooth amber beads…
senses.see: What is the most incredible thing right in front of you right now…

stories.question: How was Warsaw's Old Town rebuilt after WWII?
stories.body: During WWII, Warsaw was deliberately destroyed by the Nazis after the Warsaw Uprising in 1944. About 85% of the city was levelled — including the entire historic Old Town, which had stood for 700 years. After the war Poles refused to abandon their old centre. Using 18th-century paintings by Bernardo Bellotto and old photographs as guides, they painstakingly rebuilt the medieval town square brick by brick, often with the original (rescued) bricks. The reconstruction was so faithful that UNESCO added it to the World Heritage list — usually you have to be 100+ years old to qualify, but Warsaw was added as an exceptional case.
stories.thinkingQuestion: If your hometown was destroyed, would you rebuild exactly the same — or build something new?

stories.question: Why does Kraków have a dragon at the foot of the castle?
stories.body: The Wawel Dragon (Smok Wawelski) is a legend over 1,000 years old. A dragon lived in a cave under Wawel Hill in Kraków, terrorising the city. The king offered his daughter's hand to anyone who could kill it. Many knights died trying. A poor shoemaker's apprentice named Krak filled a sheep skin with sulphur, set it outside the cave, and the dragon swallowed it. Burning inside, the dragon drank from the Vistula River until he burst. Today a metal dragon statue stands by the river — and breathes real fire every few minutes when you SMS the right number.

stories.question: There's a whole town carved out of salt under Poland?
stories.body: The Wieliczka Salt Mine near Kraków has been worked continuously for 700 years. Polish miners didn't just dig out salt — over centuries they carved an entire underground world. There are 245 km of tunnels going 327 m down. Inside: a giant chapel with chandeliers made of rock salt, statues of saints, even an entire underground lake. The miners carved beauty out of their workplace because they spent their whole lives down there. The mine still produces salt today, and you can tour the upper levels — everything you touch is salty.

stories.question: Why has Poland's shape kept changing on maps for 1,000 years?
stories.body: Poland is a country with one of the most dramatic histories in Europe. In the 1500s it was one of the biggest countries on the continent. Then in the 1790s its neighbours (Russia, Prussia, and Austria) carved it up between them three times, and Poland disappeared from the map completely for 123 years. It came back in 1918 after WWI, was invaded by both Germany and the Soviet Union in 1939, and after WWII had its entire western border moved 200 km west (Germany lost land to Poland; Poland lost land to the USSR). Poland today is roughly the same shape and size as 1,000 years ago — but it took a lot of suffering to get back here.

stories.question: What was Solidarity and why did a shipyard worker win the Nobel Peace Prize?
stories.body: Through the 1980s, communist Poland was ruled by a Soviet-backed government. In August 1980, shipyard workers in Gdańsk went on strike, demanding better conditions and the right to form an independent trade union. Their leader was Lech Wałęsa, an electrician. The union they formed — Solidarność (Solidarity) — grew to 10 million members (a quarter of the Polish population). The government tried to crush them with martial law, but Solidarity went underground and eventually negotiated free elections in 1989, ending communist rule in Poland. This triggered the fall of communism across the rest of Eastern Europe within months. Wałęsa won the Nobel Peace Prize in 1983 and later became President of Poland.
stories.thinkingQuestion: A trade union of ordinary workers triggered the fall of an empire. What does that tell you about ordinary people?

convoQuestions.question: What's the best thing you've eaten in Poland so far?
Pierogi — meat, potato-and-cheese, or sweet? Which would you order again?
Kraków or Warsaw — which would you choose for a longer stay?
Poland is one of the cheapest countries in Europe. What's the most surprising price you've seen?
What's one Polish habit you'd like to bring home?
Poland disappeared from the map for 123 years and came back. What does that tell us about identity and nations?

wordSearch.hint: Polish foods, places and things to spot.

animals.name: White stork
animals.description: Poland has more storks than any other country — about 50,000 nesting pairs. They're seen as good luck.

animals.name: European bison (żubr)
animals.description: Europe's largest land mammal — about 800 live wild in Białowieża Forest. The species was saved from extinction here.

animals.name: Wild boar
animals.description: Increasingly common — even in Polish city parks at night.

animals.name: Red deer
animals.description: In forests across the country. You can hear males roaring in autumn.

animals.name: Honey bee
animals.description: Polish beekeepers produce 25,000 tonnes of honey a year. Wooden bee hives in countryside.

animals.name: Brown bear
animals.description: About 100 in the Tatra and Bieszczady mountains. Strictly protected.

animals.name: Grey wolf
animals.description: Around 3,000 in Poland — biggest population in central Europe. Hunting them is illegal.
